name: Afghanistan
fact: Afghanistan has been invaded by basically every empire in history, each one leaving confused and slightly poorer.

name: Albania
fact: Albania has more Mercedes-Benz cars per capita than almost any country on Earth, despite being one of Europe's poorest nations.

name: Algeria
fact: Algeria is the largest country in Africa, yet 90% of its population lives on just 12% of the land because the rest is the Sahara Desert.

name: Andorra
fact: Andorra has no army and hasn't fought a war since 1278, making it the world's most successful pacifist microstate run by two co-princes.

name: Angola
fact: Luanda was once ranked the most expensive city in the world for expats, yet most locals live on under $2 a day.

name: Antigua and Barbuda
fact: Antigua boasts 365 beaches, one for every day of the year, which is either brilliant marketing or a very suspicious coincidence.

name: Argentina
fact: Argentina has had so many economic crises that its citizens are basically professional financial disaster survivors.

name: Armenia
fact: Armenia claims to be the world's first Christian nation, which they bring up approximately every 10 minutes in conversation.

name: Australia
fact: Australia lost a war to emus in 1932 and the emus won. This is an actual historical event the Australian government prefers not to discuss.

name: Austria
fact: Austria gave the world both Mozart and Hitler, and Austrians have been trying to balance that cultural ledger ever since.

name: Azerbaijan
fact: Azerbaijan has mud volcanoes that occasionally catch fire, creating flaming mud geysers that are somehow not the country's biggest tourist attraction.

name: Bahamas
fact: The Bahamas has an island called Pig Beach populated entirely by swimming pigs who will aggressively demand your lunch.

name: Bahrain
fact: Bahrain is so small you can drive across the entire country in 30 minutes, yet it somehow hosts a Formula 1 Grand Prix.

name: Bangladesh
fact: Dhaka is one of the most densely populated cities on Earth, where personal space is considered a Western luxury.

name: Barbados
fact: Barbados is the birthplace of rum and takes this responsibility extremely seriously, with more rum shops per square mile than anywhere on Earth.

name: Belarus
fact: Belarus is often called Europe's last dictatorship, which is harsh but the current president has been in power since 1994 so it's hard to argue.

name: Belgium
fact: Belgium once went 589 days without a government and nobody really noticed because the bureaucracy just kept running itself.

name: Belize
fact: Belize moved its capital after a hurricane destroyed the old one, making it one of the few countries to relocate due to bad weather.

name: Benin
fact: Benin is considered the birthplace of Voodoo, the actual religious practice, not the Hollywood version involving dolls and pins.

name: Bhutan
fact: Bhutan measures its success in Gross National Happiness instead of GDP, which sounds lovely until you realise tourism is strictly limited and expensive.

name: Bolivia
fact: Bolivia has two capitals: Sucre is the constitutional one and La Paz is the actual working one, because one capital is simply not enough confusion.

name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
fact: Bosnia and Herzegovina has three presidents who rotate every 8 months, which is either very democratic or absolutely chaotic.

name: Botswana
fact: Botswana went from one of the world's poorest countries at independence to an upper-middle-income nation, largely thanks to diamonds and competent governance.

name: Brazil
fact: Brazil built an entirely new capital city from scratch in just 41 months, then made it look like a spaceship from above because why not.

name: Brunei
fact: The Sultan of Brunei owns over 7,000 cars including 600 Rolls-Royces, which raises questions about where he parks them all.

name: Bulgaria
fact: Bulgarians shake their head to mean yes and nod to mean no, which has caused enormous confusion for every tourist who has ever been there.

name: Burkina Faso
fact: Burkina Faso's capital Ouagadougou is widely considered one of the most fun city names to say out loud, which is genuinely one of its best features.

name: Burundi
fact: Burundi changed its capital from Bujumbura to Gitega in 2019, which is remarkable mainly because Bujumbura is a much more satisfying word.

name: Cabo Verde
fact: Cabo Verde has no indigenous population — everyone there is descended from Portuguese colonists and African slaves, making it one of the world's most intentionally created nations.

name: Cambodia
fact: Cambodia is the only country whose flag features a building, Angkor Wat, which is fair enough as it's genuinely spectacular.

name: Cameroon
fact: Cameroon is called Africa in miniature because it contains basically every climate and landscape the continent has to offer, crammed into one country.

name: Canada
fact: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined and somehow still manages to be polite about it.

name: Central African Republic
fact: The CAR once had an emperor who crowned himself in a $30 million ceremony, which was roughly the country's entire annual budget.

name: Chad
fact: Chad's flag is almost identical to Romania's flag, a situation both countries are apparently fine with since neither has done anything about it.

name: Chile
fact: Chile is the longest and narrowest country in the world — 4,300km long but only 350km wide — like someone stretched it on a rack.

name: China
fact: China has a single time zone despite spanning five geographical ones, meaning sunrise in western China can happen at 10am.

name: Colombia
fact: Colombia is the only country in South America with coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea, which it is very smug about.

name: Comoros
fact: Comoros has had over 20 coups or coup attempts since independence in 1975, averaging roughly one coup every two years.

name: Congo, Republic of the
fact: Brazzaville and Kinshasa are two capitals directly facing each other across the Congo River, making them the closest pair of capitals in the world.

name: Congo, Democratic Republic of the
fact: The DRC contains enough hydroelectric potential to power the entire African continent, yet most of its citizens have no electricity.

name: Costa Rica
fact: Costa Rica abolished its military in 1948 and spent the defence budget on education and healthcare instead, which turned out to be a great decision.

name: Croatia
fact: Croatia invented the necktie, which means every office worker silently cursing their Monday morning outfit has Croatia to blame.

name: Cuba
fact: Cuba has one of the highest doctor-to-patient ratios in the world and exports doctors to other countries, which is either admirable or suspicious.

name: Cyprus
fact: Nicosia is the last divided capital city in the world, split by a UN buffer zone that cuts straight through the city centre.

name: Czech Republic
fact: The Czech Republic drinks more beer per capita than any other country on Earth, which they consider a point of national pride.

name: Denmark
fact: Denmark consistently ranks as the happiest country in the world despite weather that would make anyone miserable, suggesting Danes are either lying or very good at hygge.

name: Djibouti
fact: Djibouti hosts military bases from the USA, France, China, Japan and Italy simultaneously, making it the world's most diplomatically crowded small nation.

name: Dominica
fact: Dominica's flag features the Sisserou parrot, making it one of the very few national flags in the world to feature the colour purple.

name: Dominican Republic
fact: The Dominican Republic shares the island of Hispaniola with Haiti, making it one of only a handful of islands shared by two completely different countries.

name: Ecuador
fact: Ecuador is named after the equator but the actual equator doesn't pass through the capital Quito, which is a geographical embarrassment nobody mentions.

name: Egypt
fact: Egypt is 96% desert, meaning almost the entire population of 100 million people is crammed along a thin strip of land beside the Nile.

name: El Salvador
fact: El Salvador became the first country to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender in 2021, a decision that has not gone entirely as planned.

name: Equatorial Guinea
fact: Equatorial Guinea is the only Spanish-speaking country in Africa, which confuses people geographically and linguistically in equal measure.

name: Eritrea
fact: Eritrea is sometimes called the North Korea of Africa due to its closed-off nature, which the government presumably considers a compliment.

name: Estonia
fact: Estonia is so digitally advanced you can vote, file taxes, start a business and access medical records online — basically everything except get a coffee.

name: Eswatini
fact: Eswatini is one of the world's last absolute monarchies, and the king renamed the country on his 50th birthday because it was his country and he felt like it.

name: Ethiopia
fact: Ethiopia has its own calendar with 13 months and is currently about 7 years behind the rest of the world — Ethiopians are literally living in the past.

name: Fiji
fact: Fiji is made up of 332 islands but only 110 are inhabited, leaving over 200 perfectly good islands sitting empty like unsold houses.

name: Finland
fact: Finland has the most heavy metal bands per capita of any country in the world, which seems at odds with their reputation for quiet, introverted politeness.

name: France
fact: France is the most visited country in the world, yet the French have somehow maintained an air of mild contempt for tourists throughout.

name: Gabon
fact: Gabon has declared it will never cut more forest than it regrows, making it one of the few carbon-negative nations on Earth.

name: Gambia
fact: Gambia is almost entirely surrounded by Senegal, like a country that got swallowed by another country.

name: Georgia
fact: Georgia claims to be the birthplace of wine, with evidence going back 8,000 years, which explains why they take viticulture so personally.

name: Germany
fact: Germany has over 1,500 different types of beer and 300 types of bread, suggesting Germans have very strong opinions about carbohydrates.

name: Ghana
fact: Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence and named itself after a medieval empire that wasn't even in the same location, for inspirational purposes.

name: Greece
fact: Greece has the longest coastline in the Mediterranean yet its economy has spent most of the 21st century underwater, which is ironic.

name: Grenada
fact: Grenada produces roughly 20% of the world's nutmeg, which is a lot of nutmeg for a country of 100,000 people.

name: Guatemala
fact: Guatemala has 37 volcanoes, three of which are currently active, suggesting Guatemalans have a very relaxed relationship with geological risk.

name: Guinea
fact: Guinea contains over half of the world's bauxite reserves yet remains one of the world's poorest countries.

name: Guinea-Bissau
fact: Guinea-Bissau has had more successful coups than democratic elections since independence, making it something of a world leader in political instability.

name: Guyana
fact: Guyana is the only English-speaking country in South America, which means it gets left off South American maps with suspicious frequency.

name: Haiti
fact: Haiti was the first country founded by formerly enslaved people after a successful revolution, which France then charged them reparations for.

name: Honduras
fact: Honduras and El Salvador once fought a war triggered by a football match, proving sports rivalries can have catastrophic consequences.

name: Hungary
fact: Hungary invented the Rubik's Cube, the ballpoint pen, and the hologram, making it responsible for three of history's most mind-bending inventions.

name: Iceland
fact: Iceland has no mosquitoes whatsoever, making it genuinely one of the most pleasant countries to visit in summer without dousing yourself in DEET.

name: India
fact: India has 22 official languages, and the government's paperwork is available in all of them, which is both impressive and a logistical nightmare.

name: Indonesia
fact: Indonesia is moving its capital from Jakarta to a brand new city because Jakarta is sinking into the sea at an alarming rate.

name: Iran
fact: Iran invented chess, algebra and the postage stamp, yet somehow the postage stamp is the one that never gets mentioned in history class.

name: Iraq
fact: Iraq is home to Mesopotamia, the Cradle of Civilisation, where humans invented writing, the wheel and cities — an impressive hat-trick.

name: Ireland
fact: Ireland has more Nobel Prize winners per capita than almost any other country, which surprised everyone including the Irish.

name: Israel
fact: Israel has the highest number of museums per capita in the world, suggesting nobody can agree on what to do with all the history.

name: Italy
fact: Italy has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country, meaning Italians are essentially living inside a museum they're not allowed to renovate.

name: Jamaica
fact: Jamaica has never won a Winter Olympics gold medal but dominates the Summer Olympics in sprinting, proving climate is destiny.

name: Japan
fact: Japan has vending machines that sell everything from hot meals to neckties, meaning you could theoretically survive entirely on vending machine purchases.

name: Jordan
fact: Jordan is home to the Dead Sea, the lowest point on Earth, where you float effortlessly because the water is so salty it essentially refuses to let you drown.

name: Kazakhstan
fact: Kazakhstan is the world's largest landlocked country and has renamed its capital three times in 25 years, suggesting a deep national uncertainty about identity.

name: Kenya
fact: Kenya has produced some of the greatest long-distance runners in history, leading scientists to study whether the entire country is simply built differently.

name: Kiribati
fact: Kiribati is the first country to see each new year and also the first likely to disappear due to rising sea levels, making it symbolically significant in two very different ways.

name: Kosovo
fact: Kosovo declared independence in 2008 and is recognised by about 100 countries, meaning roughly 90 countries still pretend it doesn't exist.

name: Kuwait
fact: Kuwait has no income tax, no sales tax and heavily subsidised petrol, which sounds perfect until you check the summer temperature of 50 degrees.

name: Kyrgyzstan
fact: Kyrgyzstan's flag features a yurt's roof viewed from the inside, making it possibly the only flag designed from the perspective of someone lying in a tent.

name: Laos
fact: Laos is the most heavily bombed country per capita in history, with the US dropping more bombs on it than on Germany and Japan combined in WWII.

name: Latvia
fact: Latvia's flag is one of the oldest in the world, with records of its use going back to 1280, predating most countries that use it as inspiration.

name: Lebanon
fact: Lebanon has 18 officially recognised religious sects and allocates government positions by religion, which works about as well as you'd expect.

name: Lesotho
fact: Lesotho is one of only three countries entirely surrounded by a single other country — South Africa — making it the world's most surrounded nation.

name: Liberia
fact: Liberia was founded by freed American slaves in 1847 and modelled its constitution on the United States, then proceeded to have a completely different experience.

name: Libya
fact: Libya had the world's simplest national flag from 1977 to 2011 — just a solid green rectangle — which required very little design effort but a lot of explanation.

name: Liechtenstein
fact: Liechtenstein discovered at the 1936 Olympics that its flag was identical to Haiti's, so it added a crown — suggesting nobody had checked beforehand.

name: Lithuania
fact: Lithuania was the last country in Europe to officially convert to Christianity, holding out until 1387, which is impressively stubborn.

name: Luxembourg
fact: Luxembourg has the highest GDP per capita in the world and its citizens have apparently agreed not to talk about this too loudly in front of their neighbours.

name: Madagascar
fact: Over 90% of Madagascar's wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth, making it essentially a separate evolutionary experiment on the same planet.

name: Malawi
fact: Malawi is called the Warm Heart of Africa due to its exceptionally friendly population, which is a nicer national brand than most countries manage.

name: Malaysia
fact: Malaysia's flag looks remarkably like the American flag, which is either a coincidence or a very bold design choice that nobody has questioned loudly enough.

name: Maldives
fact: The Maldives is the lowest-lying country in the world at 2.4 metres maximum elevation, meaning a slightly ambitious wave could theoretically end the nation.

name: Mali
fact: Mali was once home to Timbuktu, a medieval centre of scholarship so remote that 'from here to Timbuktu' became synonymous with the middle of nowhere.

name: Malta
fact: Valletta is the smallest EU capital and has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites per square metre than anywhere else on Earth.

name: Marshall Islands
fact: The Marshall Islands had 67 nuclear weapons detonated on it by the United States between 1946 and 1958, which the Marshall Islands considers a significant overstep.

name: Mauritania
fact: Mauritania only criminalised slavery in 2007, making it one of the last countries on Earth to take that particular step.

name: Mauritius
fact: Mauritius was the only known home of the dodo bird, which it managed to drive to extinction within 80 years of first encountering it.

name: Mexico
fact: Mexico City is built on an ancient lake bed, which is why it sinks about 50cm every year and experiences earthquakes with unusual enthusiasm.

name: Micronesia
fact: Micronesia uses the US dollar as its currency but has no say in US monetary policy, which is an arrangement the US finds perfectly reasonable.

name: Moldova
fact: Moldova is consistently ranked as Europe's poorest country, yet somehow produces some of the continent's most celebrated wine.

name: Monaco
fact: Monaco is the second smallest country in the world and the most densely populated, meaning its entire population could fit in a large shopping centre.

name: Mongolia
fact: Mongolia is the most sparsely populated country in the world with roughly 2 people per square kilometre, meaning the horses outnumber the humans.

name: Montenegro
fact: Montenegro adopted the Euro without being in the Eurozone or the EU, which the European Central Bank finds both flattering and slightly irritating.

name: Morocco
fact: Morocco is home to the University of Al Quaraouiyine, founded in 859 AD, widely considered the world's oldest continuously operating university.

name: Mozambique
fact: Mozambique's flag features an AK-47 assault rifle, making it the only national flag to depict a specific modern weapon.

name: Myanmar
fact: Myanmar built a brand new capital in the middle of nowhere in 2006, reportedly because a military astrologer told the generals to move.

name: Namibia
fact: Namibia has one of the lowest population densities in the world, meaning you could drive for hours without seeing another human being.

name: Nauru
fact: Nauru got extremely rich from phosphate mining, blew all the money, and now survives partly by selling passports and hosting offshore detention centres.

name: Nepal
fact: Nepal is the only country with a non-rectangular flag — it's a double pennant shape that required a special mathematical formula to standardise.

name: Netherlands
fact: A quarter of the Netherlands sits below sea level, held back by dykes that the Dutch maintain with impressive engineering and quiet anxiety.

name: New Zealand
fact: New Zealand was the first country to give women the right to vote in 1893, and has spent 130 years wondering why everyone else took so long.

name: Nicaragua
fact: Nicaragua is the largest country in Central America yet somehow the least visited, which its tourism board is working very hard to change.

name: Niger
fact: Niger is the largest country in West Africa with over 80% of its land covered by the Sahara Desert, leaving not much room for anything else.

name: Nigeria
fact: Nigeria has the second largest film industry in the world by volume, producing more films annually than Hollywood.

name: North Korea
fact: North Korea created its own time zone called Pyongyang Time in 2015 to mark liberation from Japan, then quietly reversed it in 2018.

name: North Macedonia
fact: North Macedonia had to add the word North to its name after a 27-year dispute with Greece about who owns the historical brand of Macedonia.

name: Norway
fact: Norway has a sovereign wealth fund worth over a trillion dollars, meaning every Norwegian citizen is technically a millionaire on paper.

name: Oman
fact: Oman is one of the most stable countries in the Middle East, achieved through oil wealth, diplomacy and not picking fights with anyone.

name: Pakistan
fact: Pakistan is home to K2, the second highest and most dangerous mountain in the world, which Pakistan is oddly proud of.

name: Palau
fact: Palau has a lake full of non-stinging jellyfish you can swim through, which sounds like a fever dream but is a genuine tourist attraction.

name: Panama
fact: Panama hats are not from Panama — they're from Ecuador. They got the name because they were shipped through Panama during canal construction.

name: Papua New Guinea
fact: Papua New Guinea has over 800 languages within its borders, representing roughly 12% of all languages on Earth.

name: Paraguay
fact: Paraguay has different emblems on each side of its flag, making it one of the very few non-symmetrical national flags in the world.

name: Peru
fact: Peru is home to Machu Picchu, built at 2,430 metres altitude without wheels, iron tools or mortar, just to make modern architects feel inadequate.

name: Philippines
fact: The Philippines flag is flown upside down during wartime as a built-in distress signal, which is either clever or an accident waiting to happen.

name: Poland
fact: Poland has been invaded, partitioned and erased from maps multiple times throughout history, yet keeps coming back, making it Europe's most resilient nation.

name: Portugal
fact: Portugal is the oldest nation-state in Europe with borders essentially unchanged since 1139, which it maintains is stability rather than stubbornness.

name: Qatar
fact: Qatar spent $220 billion hosting a football World Cup, including air-conditioning outdoor stadiums in 40 degree heat.

name: Romania
fact: Romania has Dracula, Nadia Comaneci and the world's heaviest building, which required demolishing a fifth of Bucharest's old city to construct.

name: Russia
fact: Russia spans 11 time zones, meaning you can celebrate New Year's Eve 11 times in a single trip across the country if you move fast enough.

name: Rwanda
fact: Rwanda has a national day of community cleaning called Umuganda, and plastic bags are completely banned, making it one of Africa's cleanest countries.

name: Saint Kitts and Nevis
fact: Saint Kitts and Nevis is the smallest sovereign state in the Western hemisphere, yet has a full seat at the United Nations.

name: Saint Lucia
fact: Saint Lucia has produced two Nobel Prize winners per capita, giving it the highest Nobel Prize rate of any country on Earth.

name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
fact: Saint Vincent was the filming location for Pirates of the Caribbean, which the island considers its greatest cultural contribution.

name: Samoa
fact: Samoa skipped the 29th of December 2011 entirely, jumping from Thursday to Saturday to align with its main trading partners.

name: San Marino
fact: San Marino claims to be the world's oldest republic, founded in 301 AD, and has survived being surrounded by Italy for 1,700 years through stubbornness.

name: São Tomé and Príncipe
fact: São Tomé and Príncipe straddles both the equator and the prime meridian, making it the closest country to coordinates 0,0 on Earth.

name: Saudi Arabia
fact: Saudi Arabia only allowed women to drive in 2018, a full century after most of the world, and announced it as a major progressive reform.

name: Senegal
fact: Senegal has never had a military coup in its entire history, making it a startling exception in a region where they are otherwise quite common.

name: Serbia
fact: Serbia is the world's second largest exporter of raspberries, which is a fact so surprising that most people assume it's wrong until they check.

name: Seychelles
fact: Seychelles has the world's smallest capital city by population with only 28,000 people, yet it still somehow manages to have traffic jams.

name: Sierra Leone
fact: Sierra Leone's capital is called Freetown because it was founded as a settlement for freed slaves, making it either a hopeful name or an ironic one.

name: Singapore
fact: Singapore fines you for chewing gum, jaywalking, and not flushing a public toilet, making it the world's most aggressively tidy city-state.

name: Slovakia
fact: Bratislava is one of the few capitals that borders two different countries — Austria and Hungary — making it conveniently located for day trips.

name: Slovenia
fact: Slovenia has the highest percentage of forest cover of any European country outside Finland and Sweden, yet somehow nobody talks about it.

name: Solomon Islands
fact: The Solomon Islands consists of 992 islands, most of which are unnamed, making its postal system an ongoing logistical adventure.

name: Somalia
fact: Somalia has been without a stable central government for so long it has accidentally become a case study in how societies function without states.

name: South Africa
fact: South Africa has three capital cities for executive, legislative and judicial functions, because choosing just one felt too decisive.

name: South Korea
fact: South Korea has the fastest average internet speeds in the world and considers slow Wi-Fi approximately a human rights issue.

name: South Sudan
fact: South Sudan is the world's newest country, gaining independence in 2011, and has since been working through a comprehensive list of nation-building challenges.

name: Spain
fact: Spain has a festival called La Tomatina where participants hurl 150,000 tomatoes at each other for no reason other than it being tremendous fun.

name: Sri Lanka
fact: Sri Lanka has the longest official capital name in the world at 22 characters, which is why most people just say Colombo and move on.

name: Sudan
fact: Khartoum sits at the exact confluence of the Blue Nile and White Nile, making it one of the most geographically significant cities in Africa.

name: Suriname
fact: Suriname is the smallest country in South America and the only Dutch-speaking country on the continent, which confuses people on two levels simultaneously.

name: Sweden
fact: Sweden has a law giving everyone the right to roam freely across any land including private property, which landowners have mixed feelings about.

name: Switzerland
fact: Switzerland has four official languages, a square national flag, and has stayed neutral in every war for 200 years, suggesting the Swiss have life figured out.

name: Syria
fact: Damascus is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, with settlement dating back 11,000 years, giving it a lot of context for current events.

name: Taiwan
fact: Taiwan produces over 90% of the world's most advanced semiconductor chips, making it one of the most geopolitically important small islands in history.

name: Tajikistan
fact: Tajikistan is 93% mountains, making it one of the most dramatic countries on Earth and an infrastructure engineer's worst nightmare.

name: Tanzania
fact: Tanzania is home to Kilimanjaro, which thousands of tourists attempt to climb each year despite having no mountaineering experience whatsoever.

name: Thailand
fact: Bangkok's full ceremonial name is 168 characters long and holds the Guinness record for longest city name, which Thais can recite and tourists cannot.

name: Timor-Leste
fact: Timor-Leste is one of the world's youngest countries, gaining independence in 2002, and has spent the years since demonstrating that nation-building is genuinely difficult.

name: Togo
fact: Togo is only 115km wide at its widest point, meaning you could drive across it sideways before finishing a podcast episode.

name: Tonga
fact: Tonga is the only Pacific nation never formally colonised by a European power, achieved through a combination of diplomacy and strategic timing.

name: Trinidad and Tobago
fact: Trinidad and Tobago invented the steel pan drum, the only new acoustic instrument family to be invented in the entire 20th century.

name: Tunisia
fact: Tunisia was the only country to emerge from the Arab Spring with a functioning democracy, which lasted about a decade before taking a complicated turn.

name: Turkey
fact: Turkey changed its name to Türkiye in 2022, partly to avoid association with the large bird that shares its old English name.

name: Turkmenistan
fact: Turkmenistan has a flaming gas crater called the Door to Hell that has been burning continuously since Soviet engineers accidentally set it on fire in 1971.

name: Tuvalu
fact: Tuvalu earns significant income by leasing its internet domain .tv to television companies, making it the only country whose economy depends on a clever URL.

name: Uganda
fact: Uganda has the youngest population of any country on Earth, with over half its citizens under 15 years old, giving it a very energetic national demographic.

name: Ukraine
fact: Ukraine is the largest country entirely within Europe and one of the world's biggest grain exporters, earning it the title breadbasket of Europe.

name: United Arab Emirates
fact: The UAE has a ski slope inside a shopping mall in the middle of a desert, which captures something essential about the country's philosophy.

name: United Kingdom
fact: The UK has an unwritten constitution, a monarch with theoretical powers nobody actually uses, and queues so orderly they're considered a cultural export.

name: United States
fact: The US is the only country to have used nuclear weapons in warfare and the only one to have landed on the moon, giving it a unique combination of achievements to reflect on.

name: Uruguay
fact: Uruguay was the first country to fully legalise marijuana at a national level in 2013, which neighbouring countries noted with interest.

name: Uzbekistan
fact: Uzbekistan is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world — surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.

name: Vanuatu
fact: Vanuatu is the bungee jumping capital of the world, derived from a ritual where men jump from wooden towers with vines tied to their ankles.

name: Vatican City
fact: Vatican City publishes its own newspaper, radio station and TV channel for a population of about 800 people, which is an impressive media operation.

name: Venezuela
fact: Venezuela has the world's largest proven oil reserves yet experienced hyperinflation so severe its currency became essentially decorative.

name: Vietnam
fact: Vietnam defeated the French, the Americans and the Chinese in war within living memory, which gives its citizens a somewhat justified sense of confidence.

name: Yemen
fact: Sana'a is one of the highest capital cities in the world at 2,300 metres and one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth.

name: Zambia
fact: Zambia and Zimbabwe share Victoria Falls and have been arguing about the exact border through it ever since.

name: Zimbabwe
fact: Zimbabwe once issued a 100 trillion dollar banknote during hyperinflation, which was worth roughly enough to buy two loaves of bread.

name: Anguilla
fact: Anguilla is a British territory so small and peaceful that its only revolution in 1967 was against joining a federation with neighbouring islands, not against Britain.

name: Antarctica
fact: Antarctica has no government, no permanent residents, and no capital city, yet seven countries have staked territorial claims on it anyway, just in case.

name: American Samoa
fact: American Samoa is the only US territory south of the equator and produces a disproportionate number of NFL players for its tiny population of 55,000.

name: Aruba
fact: Aruba sits outside the hurricane belt which is either very lucky or very good forward planning by whoever chose to colonise it.

name: Åland Islands
fact: The Åland Islands are Finnish but Swedish-speaking, demilitarised by international treaty, and have their own parliament and postal stamps, making them Finland's most autonomous archipelago.

name: Saint Barthélemy
fact: Saint Barthélemy is a French Caribbean island named after Christopher Columbus's brother, which is exactly the kind of sibling favour that lasts forever.

name: Bermuda
fact: Bermuda is the most northerly coral reef in the world and gave its name to a triangle of ocean where ships and planes supposedly disappear, which has been greatly exaggerated.

name: Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba
fact: These three tiny Dutch islands in the Caribbean are officially municipalities of the Netherlands, meaning residents can technically say they live in Europe while standing in the Caribbean.

name: Bouvet Island
fact: Bouvet Island is the most remote island on Earth, closer to Antarctica than to any inhabited land, and is owned by Norway despite being nowhere near Norway.

name: Cocos (Keeling) Islands
fact: The Cocos Islands are an Australian territory in the Indian Ocean where the entire population could fit comfortably inside a moderately sized supermarket.

name: Côte d'Ivoire
fact: Côte d'Ivoire has the world's largest church by area — the Basilica of Our Lady of Peace — built in the middle of nowhere by the first president, who modelled it on St Peter's in Rome.

name: Cook Islands
fact: The Cook Islands are in free association with New Zealand, meaning residents can use New Zealand passports but New Zealand has no say in their internal affairs, which suits everyone.

name: Curaçao
fact: Curaçao gave its name to the famous blue liqueur made from the dried peel of a bitter orange that only grows there, making it the only island with its own cocktail ingredient.

name: Christmas Island
fact: Christmas Island has an annual red crab migration where 50 million crabs cross roads and beaches to reach the sea, at which point the whole island essentially stops.

name: Western Sahara
fact: Western Sahara is the world's most sparsely populated territory and has been disputed between Morocco and the Sahrawi independence movement since Spain left in 1976.

name: Falkland Islands
fact: Argentina and the United Kingdom fought a war over the Falkland Islands in 1982, an archipelago most people couldn't have found on a map beforehand.

name: Faroe Islands
fact: The Faroe Islands have their own football team ranked higher than many actual countries, and their own language spoken by only 50,000 people who refuse to abandon it.

name: England
fact: England invented football, cricket, tennis and rugby, then proceeded to be defeated at all of them by countries that took the games more seriously.

name: Northern Ireland
fact: Northern Ireland has a peace wall in Belfast longer than the Berlin Wall ever was, separating communities that have been neighbours for centuries.

name: Scotland
fact: Scotland's national animal is the unicorn, which was chosen for its symbolism of power and independence, and also because nobody told them they had to pick a real animal.

name: Wales
fact: Wales has a dragon on its national flag, making it the only country in the world whose flag features a mythical creature, which is an objectively excellent decision.

name: French Guiana
fact: French Guiana is technically part of France and the EU, meaning the Amazon rainforest is legally European territory and you can use Euros to buy things there.

name: Guernsey
fact: Guernsey is a British Crown Dependency that is not part of the UK or the EU but has its own parliament, legal system and very competitive tax rates.

name: Gibraltar
fact: Gibraltar is a tiny British territory at the tip of Spain that Spain has been asking for back since 1713, with Britain politely declining every single time.

name: Greenland
fact: Greenland is the world's largest island and is mostly covered in ice, while Iceland — despite the name — is mostly green, in a geographical naming mix-up nobody has ever fixed.

name: Guadeloupe
fact: Guadeloupe is a French Caribbean island that is officially part of France, meaning its residents vote in French elections despite living 7,000km from Paris.

name: South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands
fact: South Georgia has a permanent human population of about 30 scientists and is home to more penguins and elephant seals than you could reasonably count.

name: Guam
fact: Guam is a US territory in the Pacific whose residents are US citizens but cannot vote in presidential elections, an arrangement they have feelings about.

name: Hong Kong
fact: Hong Kong was a British colony until 1997 when it was handed back to China under a one country, two systems arrangement scheduled to last 50 years.

name: Heard Island and McDonald Islands
fact: Heard Island contains one of Australia's two active volcanoes, which is surprising because most people didn't know Australia had any active volcanoes at all.

name: Isle of Man
fact: The Isle of Man has the world's oldest continuously running parliament, the Tynwald, established by Vikings in 979 AD, and hosts the famously dangerous TT motorcycle race.

name: British Indian Ocean Territory
fact: The British Indian Ocean Territory's entire indigenous population was relocated to Mauritius in the 1960s to make way for a US military base, a fact the UK government still finds uncomfortable to discuss.

name: Jersey
fact: Jersey is a British Crown Dependency closer to France than to England, with its own parliament, currency and legal system, and very strong opinions about its cows.

name: Cayman Islands
fact: The Cayman Islands has more registered companies than people, making it technically the world's most corporate archipelago.

name: Saint Martin (French part)
fact: The island of Saint Martin is split between France and the Netherlands with no border controls, making it the world's smallest island shared by two different countries.

name: Macau
fact: Macau has the highest gambling revenue of any territory on Earth, making Las Vegas look like a modest hobby by comparison.

name: Northern Mariana Islands
fact: The Northern Mariana Islands are a US commonwealth whose residents are US citizens but were specifically excluded from the minimum wage law for decades.

name: Martinique
fact: Martinique is an official region of France in the Caribbean, meaning it uses the Euro and follows French law, yet is closer to Venezuela than to Paris.

name: Montserrat
fact: Montserrat's original capital Plymouth was buried under volcanic ash in 1997 and remains frozen in time beneath metres of debris, making it the world's only abandoned capital.

name: New Caledonia
fact: New Caledonia has held three independence referendums from France and voted to stay each time, though the margin keeps getting closer.

name: Norfolk Island
fact: Norfolk Island was settled by the mutineers from the Bounty and their Tahitian companions, giving it arguably the most dramatic founding story of any territory on Earth.

name: Niue
fact: Niue is one of the world's largest coral islands and has such a small population that it is in free association with New Zealand partly because it cannot sustain full independence.

name: French Polynesia
fact: French Polynesia includes Tahiti and Bora Bora, which are technically part of France despite being in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, 15,000km from Paris.

name: Saint Pierre and Miquelon
fact: Saint Pierre and Miquelon are tiny French islands just off the coast of Canada, making them the last remnant of France's once enormous North American empire.

name: Pitcairn Islands
fact: The Pitcairn Islands have a population of about 50 people, all descended from Bounty mutineers, making it the world's least populous national jurisdiction.

name: Puerto Rico
fact: Puerto Rico has been a US territory since 1898 and its 3.2 million residents are US citizens who cannot vote in presidential elections unless they move to a state.

name: Palestine
fact: Palestine is recognised as a state by over 140 UN member countries but not by the US or most of Western Europe, making its international status one of the most contested in the world.

name: Réunion
fact: Réunion is a French island in the Indian Ocean with an active volcano called Piton de la Fournaise that erupts several times a year, which residents treat as a minor inconvenience.

name: Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha
fact: Saint Helena is where Napoleon was exiled after Waterloo, chosen specifically because it was one of the most remote inhabited islands on Earth.

name: Svalbard and Jan Mayen
fact: Svalbard has a global seed vault buried in permafrost containing backup copies of over a million crop varieties, essentially a Noah's Ark for plants.

name: Sint Maarten (Dutch part)
fact: Sint Maarten shares an island with French Saint Martin and the two sides have no border controls, making it technically possible to accidentally wander between two countries.

name: Turks and Caicos Islands
fact: The Turks and Caicos Islands nearly joined Canada in the 1970s, a merger that would have made them the only tropical Canadian province.

name: French Southern and Antarctic Lands
fact: The French Southern Territories include several subantarctic islands and a slice of Antarctica, giving France a territorial presence at the bottom of the world that nobody challenges mainly because nobody else wants to go there.

name: Tokelau
fact: Tokelau is a New Zealand territory with no airport, accessible only by a 36-hour boat journey from Samoa, making it one of the most isolated inhabited territories on Earth.

name: U.S. Minor Outlying Islands
fact: The US Minor Outlying Islands are a collection of mostly uninhabited Pacific and Caribbean atolls that the US accumulated during the 19th century and hasn't quite worked out what to do with since.

name: British Virgin Islands
fact: The British Virgin Islands has more registered companies per capita than almost anywhere on Earth, despite having fewer people than a mid-sized English town.

name: U.S. Virgin Islands
fact: The US bought the Virgin Islands from Denmark in 1917 for $25 million in gold, which Denmark has occasionally wondered if it should have negotiated harder on.

name: Wallis and Futuna
fact: Wallis and Futuna is a French collectivity in the Pacific that voted to remain French in a 1959 referendum and has shown little sign of reconsidering since.

name: Mayotte
fact: Mayotte voted to remain French when the Comoros islands declared independence in 1974, making it an official French department in the Indian Ocean surrounded by a foreign country.
